message.content:
Here are the **canonical, published sources/standards** you can cite for **mobile application testing** across security, quality, UX, performance, and permissions, with exact identifiers where they exist.

---

### 1. OWASP MASVS – Mobile Application Security Verification Standard

- **Name:** OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard (**MASVS**)  
- **Organization:** OWASP – OWASP Mobile Application Security (MAS) flagship project[4][6]  
- **What it is:** Baseline **security and privacy requirements** for mobile apps, used to define *what* must be tested.[1][3][4]  
- **Test / requirement categories (canonical IDs):** MASVS defines requirement groups as **MASVS-XXXXX** categories[1]:

  - **MASVS-STORAGE** – Secure storage of sensitive data on device (data-at-rest)[1].  
  - **MASVS-CRYPTO** – Cryptographic functionality used to protect sensitive data[1].  
  - **MASVS-AUTH** – Authentication and authorization mechanisms[1].  
  - **MASVS-NETWORK** – Secure network communication (data-in-transit)[1].  
  - **MASVS-PLATFORM** – Secure interaction with mobile platform and other apps[1].  
  - **MASVS-CODE** – Secure coding practices, update mechanisms and data processing[1].  
  - **MASVS-RESILIENCE** – Resilience to reverse engineering and tampering[1].  
  - **MASVS-PRIVACY** – Privacy controls for user data and privacy protection[1].

- **Identifiers structure:**  
  - Categories are referred to as **MASVS-STORAGE**, **MASVS-CRYPTO**, etc.[1].  
  - Individual requirements are numbered within each category (e.g., in v2.1.0 PDF: `MASVS-STORAGE-1`, etc.).[8]  
- **Test categories covered:**  
  - **Security:** storage, crypto, auth, network, platform, code, resilience, privacy[1][4].  
  - **Privacy:** explicit MASVS-PRIVACY[1].  
  - **Permissions:** covered implicitly via PLATFORM and PRIVACY controls (e.g., access to sensors, PII) in MASVS requirements[1][8].  
- **Official URL:** mas.owasp.org/MASVS/[1]

---

### 2. OWASP MASTG – Mobile Application Security Testing Guide

- **Name:** OWASP Mobile Application Security Testing Guide (**MASTG**, formerly MSTG)[4][5]  
- **Organization:** OWASP – OWASP MAS flagship project[4][6]  
- **What it is:** Comprehensive **manual for mobile app security testing and reverse engineering**, describing test processes and techniques, and providing **exhaustive test cases** mapped to MASVS[4][5].  
- **Role vs MASVS:**  
  - MASVS defines **requirements**; MASTG defines **how to test** those requirements through technical test cases and tools[4][5][8].  
- **Test / chapter categories:**  
  The MASTG is organized into platform-specific and topic-specific chapters (e.g., for Android, iOS, network, crypto, code tampering). Each chapter contains **MASTG test cases** with canonical IDs (e.g., `MSTG-STORAGE-1` in earlier versions; in current branding still referred to in checklist/test case mappings)[4][5][8].  
  Typical test-category groupings include (naming varies slightly per version, but identifiers follow a consistent pattern):

  - **MASTG-STORAGE** – Testing secure **local storage** of sensitive data (data at rest), mapped to **MASVS-STORAGE**[5][8].  
  - **MASTG-CRYPTO** – Testing **cryptographic** usage (key management, algorithms, randomness), mapped to **MASVS-CRYPTO**[5][8].  
  - **MASTG-AUTH** – Testing **authentication / session / authorization** aspects, mapped to **MASVS-AUTH**[4][5].  
  - **MASTG-NETWORK** – Testing **network security**, TLS usage, cert pinning, mapped to **MASVS-NETWORK**[5][8].  
  - **MASTG-PLATFORM** – Testing **platform interaction**, IPC, intents, permissions handling, platform security features, mapped to **MASVS-PLATFORM**[4][5].  
  - **MASTG-CODE** – Testing **secure coding**, input validation, error handling, code quality aspects that affect security, mapped to **MASVS-CODE**[4][5].  
  - **MASTG-RESILIENCE** – Testing **resistance to reverse engineering, tampering, debugging, hooking**, mapped to **MASVS-RESILIENCE**[4][5].  
  - **MASTG-PRIVACY** – Testing **privacy controls and data minimization**, mapped to **MASVS-PRIVACY**[4][5].

- **Identifiers structure (test IDs):**  
  - Each test case has a canonical ID in the pattern **MASTG-<CATEGORY>-<NN>** (older docs show **MSTG-** but OWASP notes that MASTG is “previously known as MSTG” and the mappings remain traceable via checklist[4][5]).  
  - Example from v2.1.0 MASVS PDF: test case IDs such as `MSTG-STORAGE-1` are referenced in the **Mobile Application Security Checklist**, which ties MASVS requirements to MASTG tests[8].  
- **Test categories covered:**  
  - **Security:** all MASVS categories (storage, crypto, auth, network, platform, code, resilience, privacy)[4][5].  
  - **Permissions:** explicit coverage in Android/iOS platform chapters (e.g., permission model, dangerous permissions, OS-level controls) via PLATFORM and PRIVACY tests[4][5].  
- **Official URL:** mas.owasp.org/MASTG/[5]

---

### 3. OWASP MASWE – Mobile Application Security Weakness Enumeration

- **Name:** OWASP Mobile Application Security Weakness Enumeration (**MASWE**)[4][6]  
- **Organization:** OWASP – OWASP MAS flagship project[4][6]  
- **What it is:** A **mobile-app-specific weakness list** that acts as the **bridge** between MASVS requirements and MASTG test cases[4].  
- **Purpose:**  
  - Enumerates common **security and privacy weaknesses** in mobile apps.  
  - Used to derive **MAS Testing Profiles** and map weaknesses to MASVS categories and MASTG tests[4][6].  
- **Identifiers:**  
  - Weaknesses have MASWE identifiers (pattern similar to other enumerations, e.g., MASWE-<NNNN>), which MASVS references as part of MAS testing profiles (described by OWASP MAS project)[4][6].  
- **Test categories covered:**  
  - **Security & privacy weaknesses** across storage, crypto, auth, network, platform, code, resilience, privacy, permissions[4][6].  
- **Official URL:** Accessible via the OWASP MAS project main page mas.owasp.org, under **MASWE**[6].

---

### 4. OWASP Mobile Application Security Checklist

- **Name:** OWASP Mobile Application Security Checklist  
- **Organization:** OWASP – OWASP MAS project[4][6]  
- **What it is:** An **Excel/CSV-style checklist** that unifies all MASVS categories into a single sheet and **links each MASVS requirement to the corresponding MASTG test cases**, with versions/commit IDs for traceability[4].  
- **Role:**  
  - Canonical, **machine- and auditor-friendly mapping** between:
    - MASVS requirement IDs (e.g., `MASVS-STORAGE-1`), and  
    - MASTG test IDs (e.g., `MSTG-STORAGE-1`, etc.), and  
    - Legacy MASVS levels (L1, L2, R) and current MAS testing profiles[1][4][8].  
- **Test categories covered:**  
  - All **MASVS categories** (STORAGE, CRYPTO, AUTH, NETWORK, PLATFORM, CODE, RESILIENCE, PRIVACY) and associated MASTG tests[4].  
- **Official URL:** Linked from OWASP MAS project page (mas.owasp.org → “Mobile Application Security Checklist”)[4][6].

---

### 5. OWASP ASVS – Application Security Verification Standard (Web / API baseline)

- **Name:** OWASP Application Security Verification Standard (**ASVS**)[7]  
- **Organization:** OWASP[7]  
- **What it is:** Standard for testing **web application** security controls; relevant for **mobile backends / APIs** consumed by mobile apps, but not mobile-specific[7].  
- **Identifiers structure:**  
  - Requirements are identified as `<chapter>.<section>.<requirement>` (e.g., `1.11.3`), and formally referenced as `v<version>-<chapter>.<section>.<requirement>`[7].  
- **Test categories covered (for services used by mobile apps):**  
  - **Authentication, session management, access control, input validation, crypto, error handling, API security**, etc.[7].  
- **Official URL:** owasp.org/www-project-application-security-verification-standard/[7]

---

### 6. React Native – Official Testing Guidance

- **Name:** React Native Testing (core React Native docs)  
- **Organization:** Meta (React Native project)  
- **What it is:** Official documentation for **testing React Native apps**, focusing on functional/quality testing rather than security. It points to recommended tools and practices.  
- **Core test categories it describes:**

  - **Unit & component testing** (logic, components, hooks) using **Jest** as the recommended test runner.[*React Native docs*]  
  - **Snapshot testing** of components.[*React Native docs*]  
  - **End-to-end (E2E) testing** with tools such as **Detox** for mobile UI automation.[*React Native docs*]  
  - Guidance on **integration testing**, mocking native modules, and how to test platform-specific code.[*React Native docs*]  

- **Identifiers:**  
  - React Native does **not** define MASVS-style IDs; tests are conventional test files/specs organized by tool (Jest, Detox).  
- **Test categories covered:**  
  - **Quality/functional correctness:** component behavior, business logic, navigation.  
  - **UX / UI behavior:** via E2E/UI tests with Detox (e.g., interaction flows, accessibility hooks).[Detox & RN docs]  
  - **Performance/security/permissions:** only high-level; security/permissions are generally delegated to platform-level guidance (Android/iOS) and not standardized like OWASP MAS.  
- **Official URL:**  
  - React Native testing documentation is on the official React Native site under “Testing” (reactnative.dev → Docs → Testing).

*(React Native docs do not define canonical security/permissions standards comparable to MASVS/MASTG; they are more about test tooling and strategy.)*

---

### 7. Expo – Official Testing Guidance for React Native Apps

- **Name:** Expo Testing (Expo Application Services docs)  
- **Organization:** Expo  
- **What it is:** Official guidance for **testing Expo-based React Native apps**, building on the React Native testing ecosystem.  
- **Core test categories it describes:**

  - How to use **Jest** for unit and component tests in Expo projects.  
  - How to use **React Native Testing Library** for component behavior and accessibility testing.  
  - How to use **Detox / E2E tools** with Expo (especially for prebuild/bare workflows).  
  - Integrations with **Expo Router** and other Expo modules in test environments.

- **Identifiers:**  
  - No MASVS-style identifiers; Expo testing is structured as configuration/documentation around standard JS/mobile test tools.  
- **Test categories covered:**  
  - **Quality / functional correctness:** components, hooks, navigation, deep links.  
  - **UX / accessibility:** via React Native Testing Library and E2E flows.  
  - **Permissions:** Expo’s *API docs* and *Permissions module* describe permission handling (e.g., camera, location), but there is **no formal permissions testing standard** akin to MASVS; they provide code examples and flows rather than test IDs.  
- **Official URL:**  
  - Expo testing docs are on the official Expo site under “Testing” (docs.expo.dev → Guides / Testing).

---

### 8. Platform-specific canonical sources for quality / UX / performance / permissions

Security-specific standards (MASVS/MASTG/MASWE) are OWASP-centric; **non-security mobile testing canon** is defined largely by platform vendor guidelines:

#### 8.1. Android

- **Android Developer Testing Documentation**  
  - **Organization:** Google  
  - **Scope:** Official guidance for **Android app testing** (including native and frameworks like React Native through the Android layer).  
  - **Test categories:**
    - **Unit tests** (JUnit, Robolectric).  
    - **Instrumentation tests / UI tests** (Espresso, UI Automator).  
    - **Performance tests** (benchmarking libraries, profiling).  
    - **Accessibility/UX testing** with Accessibility Test Framework and tools.  
  - **Permissions:**  
    - Official **“Permissions”** and **“Best practices for permissions”** docs define canonical behavior and test expectations for runtime permissions, dangerous permissions, and privacy-sensitive APIs.  
  - **Identifiers:**  
    - No MASVS-style IDs; uses code/sample-based guidance.  
  - **Official URLs:** developer.android.com → “Testing”; “App quality”; “Permissions”.

#### 8.2. iOS

- **Xcode / iOS Testing Documentation (Unit, UI, Performance)**  
  - **Organization:** Apple  
  - **Scope:** Official frameworks for **testing iOS apps**.  
  - **Test categories:**
    - **Unit tests** using XCTest.  
    - **UI tests** using XCUITest.  
    - **Performance tests** with XCTest performance metrics and Instruments.  
  - **Permissions & privacy:**  
    - **App Privacy & Permissions** guidance (e.g., “Requesting Authorization for Accessing User Data”, “Protecting the User’s Privacy”) defines what must be requested, app behavior when denied, and UX expectations.  
  - **Identifiers:**  
    - No MASVS-style IDs; tests are standard XCTest case classes and methods.  
  - **Official URLs:** developer.apple.com → Xcode → Testing; and platform-specific “Privacy & Permissions” docs.

---

### 9. How these map to your focus areas

- **Security (mobile app):**  
  - **Primary canon:** OWASP **MASVS** (requirements), **MASWE** (weaknesses), **MASTG** (test cases/processes), and the **Mobile Application Security Checklist** for MASVS–MASTG mappings[1][4][5][6][8].  
  - Identifiers: **MASVS-STORAGE**, **MASVS-CRYPTO**, **MASVS-AUTH**, **MASVS-NETWORK**, **MASVS-PLATFORM**, **MASVS-CODE**, **MASVS-RESILIENCE**, **MASVS-PRIVACY**, plus granular requirement IDs; MASTG test IDs such as **MASTG-STORAGE-n** / legacy **MSTG-STORAGE-n** mapped via the checklist[1][4][5][8].

- **Quality / functional correctness:**  
  - **React Native:** official testing docs (Jest, Detox, React Native Testing Library).  
  - **Expo:** official testing docs integrating those tools.  
  - **Native platforms:** Android and iOS testing docs (unit, UI, integration tests).

- **UX / accessibility:**  
  - **React Native & Expo:** use of React Native Testing Library and E2E tools, plus platform-specific accessibility APIs.  
  - **Android/iOS:** platform accessibility & UX testing guidance.

- **Performance:**  
  - **Android:** performance testing & benchmarking docs (Android Benchmark, Profiler).  
  - **iOS:** XCTest performance tests and Instruments.  
  - **React Native / Expo:** refer back to platform tools; some guidance on profiling JS and bridge performance.

- **Permissions & privacy:**  
  - **OWASP MASVS / MASTG / MASWE:** security/privacy requirements and tests via **MASVS-PLATFORM** and **MASVS-PRIVACY** categories and corresponding MASTG tests[1][4][5].  
  - **Android/iOS:** canonical runtime permission and privacy behavior docs.  
  - **Expo / React Native:** API-level guidance for permissions wrappers over Android/iOS (no formal test IDs).

If you want, I can next enumerate concrete examples of **MASTG test IDs** (e.g., `MSTG-STORAGE-1`, `MSTG-AUTH-2`) with their descriptions and associated **MASVS** controls for a particular category like STORAGE or NETWORK.
